entry.title: Combined summary - The Four Horsemen
entry.summary: The document titled "The Four Horsemen (of the Blockchain)" presents a unified approach to enhancing Bitcoin transaction privacy through a new specification combining several coinjoin protocols, including WabiSabi, ZeroLink, JoinMarket, and PayJoin. By integrating these technologies, the proposal aims to address existing issues such as on-chain privacy, system fragmentation, and liquidity challenges in a decentralized and efficient manner. Each subprotocol contributes distinct functionalities aimed at improving the privacy and efficiency of Bitcoin transactions. WabiSabi is designed to enable UTXO consolidation while preserving the anonymity of ownership and facilitating privacy-enhanced spending patterns. This protocol is specifically integrated into Wasabi Wallet v2.0 and beyond. ZeroLink focuses on remixing capabilities with added DoS protection, already implemented in previous versions of Wasabi Wallet. JoinMarket enhances the system by providing on-demand liquidity which is crucial for high-volume trading scenarios and incorporates advanced privacy features like Proof of Discrete Log Equivalence. On the other hand, PayJoin aims at disrupting third-party transaction analysis by involving direct transactions between users, supported across various wallet versions which can be found at [Payjoin v1](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/master/bip-0078.mediawiki) and [Payjoin v2](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/master/bip-0077.md). Despite their strengths, each subprotocol also brings specific drawbacks. For instance, WabiSabi, although scalable, faces challenges such as slower transaction speeds and increased costs per transaction. ZeroLink may limit the flexibility of transaction amounts, potentially resulting in unspendable change amounts. JoinMarket, while beneficial for passive liquidity, requires some level of trust in the taker's role. Conversely, PayJoin offers enhanced privacy but relies heavily on the participation of both parties in a transaction, introducing potential points of failure. The specification thoroughly evaluates incentive structures and user experience (UX), detailing how these subprotocols interoperate to balance out their individual weaknesses and deliver a holistic privacy solution tailored to user needs. The design not only addresses current requirements but also sets the foundation for future enhancements, requiring users to synchronize wallets either through compact block filters or a fully operational node. Open for community input, this ongoing project emphasizes continuous improvement to maximize both privacy and utility in Bitcoin transactions.
